Japanese society for cancer of the colon and rectum (JSCCR) guidelines 2024 for the clinical practice of hereditary colorectal cancer

evidence_entities.text: Approximately 5% of all colorectal cancers have a strong genetic component and are classified as hereditary colorectal cancer (HCRC)
The most common HCRCs are familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) and Lynch syndrome

Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by the presence of colorectal adenomatous polyposis, which is caused primarily by a germline pathogenic variant (GPV) in the APC gene
Lynch syndrome is a hereditary autosomal dominant disease that is primarily caused by a germline pathogenic variant (GPV) in one of the DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes
